questions.question: Which of the following imports only the 'sqrt' function from the math module?
questions.explanation: 'from math import sqrt' imports only the sqrt function into the current namespace.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'as' keyword in an import statement?
options: It creates an alias for the module
It checks if the module exists
questions.explanation: 'import module as alias' lets you refer to the module by a shorter or more convenient name.

questions.question: What is __name__ set to when a Python file is run directly?
questions.explanation: When a file is executed directly, __name__ is set to '__main__'. When imported, it is set to the module's name.

questions.question: Why should you avoid 'from module import *'?
options: It is slower than other imports
It can overwrite existing variable names
It only works with built-in modules
questions.explanation: 'from module import *' dumps everything into your namespace, which can accidentally shadow or overwrite your own variables and functions.

questions.question: Complete the code: '___ math' imports the math module so you can call math.sqrt().
Which keyword lets you give a module a shorter name when importing? (one word)
